article.title: European Commission Confirms Cyberattack on AWS-Hosted Infrastructure as Hacker Claims 350 GB Data Theft
article.summary: A threat actor breached at least one European Commission AWS account, claiming to have stolen over 350 GB of data including employee information and databases from Europa.eu infrastructure.
article.body_markdown:
## Overview

The European Commission, the executive branch of the European Union, has confirmed that a cyberattack discovered on March 24 compromised part of its cloud infrastructure hosting the Europa.eu web platform. A threat actor subsequently contacted journalists, claiming to have exfiltrated over 350 GB of data -- including multiple databases and employee information -- from at least one of the Commission's Amazon Web Services accounts, according to [BleepingComputer](https://www.bleepingcomputer.com/news/security/european-commission-investigating-breach-after-amazon-cloud-account-hack/).

The breach marks the second cybersecurity incident to hit the Commission in 2026, following a January attack on its mobile device management infrastructure that exposed staff names and phone numbers.

## What We Know

The Commission disclosed the attack on March 27, stating that it had "discovered a cyber-attack, which affected part of our cloud infrastructure" and that "immediate steps were taken to contain the attack" with "risk mitigation measures" implemented, as reported by [TechCrunch](https://techcrunch.com/2026/03/27/european-commission-confirms-cyberattack-after-hackers-claim-data-breach/) and [CSO Online](https://www.csoonline.com/article/4151363/european-commission-data-stolen-in-a-cyberattack-on-the-infrastructure-hosting-its-web-sites.html). The Commission confirmed that its internal IT systems were not compromised and that the breach was limited to cloud infrastructure hosting its public-facing Europa.eu websites.

The unnamed threat actor reached out to [BleepingComputer](https://www.bleepingcomputer.com/news/security/european-commission-investigating-breach-after-amazon-cloud-account-hack/), providing screenshots as evidence of access to Commission employee data and email server information. The attacker stated they had no intention of extorting the Commission but planned to release the stolen data publicly at a later date.

Amazon distanced itself from the incident, stating that "AWS did not experience a security event, and our services operated as designed," according to [BleepingComputer](https://www.bleepingcomputer.com/news/security/european-commission-investigating-breach-after-amazon-cloud-account-hack/). This response suggests the compromise stemmed from account-level credential abuse rather than a vulnerability in AWS infrastructure itself.

The Europa.eu websites remained operational throughout the incident, and the Commission's cybersecurity incident response team launched an investigation to determine the full scope of the breach.

## A Pattern of Incidents

The AWS breach follows a separate cyberattack detected on January 30, when the Commission identified traces of intrusion on its mobile device management platform. That earlier incident, [disclosed by BleepingComputer](https://www.bleepingcomputer.com/news/security/european-commission-discloses-breach-that-exposed-staff-data/), may have exposed staff names and mobile phone numbers. The Commission contained the January breach within nine hours and found no evidence that mobile devices themselves were compromised.

The January attack exploited vulnerabilities in Ivanti Endpoint Manager Mobile (EPMM), specifically CVE-2026-1281 and CVE-2026-1340, which are code-injection flaws allowing remote attackers to execute arbitrary code without authentication. The same vulnerabilities affected the Dutch Data Protection Authority and Finland's government IT service provider Valtori, which reported potential exposure of data belonging to 50,000 government users.

## What We Don't Know

Several critical questions remain unanswered. The Commission has not disclosed the specific breach methodology -- whether the attacker gained access through phishing, credential theft, a misconfigured service, or a zero-day vulnerability. The exact contents of the 350 GB of allegedly stolen data have not been independently verified, and the Commission has not confirmed the attacker's claims about the volume or nature of the exfiltrated information.

The identity and motivation of the threat actor also remain unclear. Security professionals cited by [CSO Online](https://www.csoonline.com/article/4151363/european-commission-data-stolen-in-a-cyberattack-on-the-infrastructure-hosting-its-web-sites.html) noted that the attacker's stated intent to release data publicly rather than seek ransom could indicate politically motivated hacktivism or state-sponsored activity. Whether this incident is connected to the January Ivanti breach has not been established.

## Analysis

The breach underscores growing concerns about Identity and Access Management (IAM) in cloud environments, particularly for high-value government targets. As [CSO Online](https://www.csoonline.com/article/4151363/european-commission-data-stolen-in-a-cyberattack-on-the-infrastructure-hosting-its-web-sites.html) noted, the incident may signal an increase in politically motivated attacks against EU institutions in 2026, at a time when the bloc is already pursuing digital sovereignty initiatives to reduce dependence on American technology platforms.

The Commission's use of AWS to host its public web infrastructure -- while its internal systems run on separate infrastructure -- appears to have limited the damage. However, the exposure of employee data and email server access raises the risk of follow-on social engineering or spear-phishing campaigns targeting Commission staff.

With two confirmed breaches in less than two months, the European Commission faces mounting pressure to demonstrate that its cybersecurity posture matches its regulatory ambitions. The EU has positioned itself as a global leader in cybersecurity regulation through the NIS2 Directive and the Cyber Resilience Act, making the security of its own institutions a matter of credibility.
